topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up, a familiar chime that usually brought a warm flush to my cheeks. It was a message from 'Starlight_Voyager,' a soul I'd connected with through obscure astrophysics forums. The words were casual, detailing a weekend trip to a new city. My stomach tightened into a hard knot. I scrolled through their recent posts, noticing the tagged locations—the same coffee shop I frequented, the park bench where I often read. A strange, prickly heat began to rise in my chest, a restless energy that made my fingers twitch on the keyboard. I wanted to know where they were, really know where they were. I typed back a carefully neutral response, my thumbs hovering over the keys, trying to keep the tremor out of my voice. It felt like watching someone walk into your orbit, suddenly occupying the space you thought was exclusively yours.

[story 2]

I was waiting in line at the bookstore, the scent of aged paper thick in the air, when my phone buzzed. It was an update from 'CosmicDrifter.' They were posting a picture of a mural downtown, a piece I recognized immediately. A sudden, sharp clench seized my jaw. I watched them move through the frame, their casual confidence radiating across the screen. My gaze drifted from the phone to the street, a tight, unfamiliar tension pulling at my shoulders. Every glance I caught of their presence in the city felt like a small, unwelcome physical jolt. I quickly pulled out my own phone, pretending to be engrossed in a book, my breath catching in my throat as I replayed the image. It was an uncomfortable feeling, this sense of being displaced, like a shadow in someone else's spotlight.

[story 3]

It started subtly. A shared comment on a local event, a seemingly random overlap in our daily routines. Then, the realization hit me like a physical blow when I saw their story—a snapshot taken from the rooftop garden of the building across the street. My hands started shaking slightly as I reread the caption, the shared geography suddenly feeling suffocatingly intimate. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I paced the room, the floorboards protesting beneath my hurried steps. There was an undeniable sense of inadequacy washing over me, a feeling that the world I thought I navigated alone had just been invaded by someone else, someone who shared my postcode. I couldn't stop staring at the screen, the vibrant life unfolding in their city feeling impossibly close, impossibly real.
